definition: Stock-bond correlation is bifurcated by maturity: short duration (2Y) maintains negative correlation (-0.25 to -0.35) providing hedge value, while long duration (30Y) shows positive correlation (+0.05 to +0.15). Hedging properties depend critically on duration selection, making this operationally essential for portfolio construction.
evidence: 2Y correlation at -0.25 to -0.35 vs 30Y at +0.05 to +0.15
Operationally critical for duration selection in hedging portfolios
Unique contribution — no other agent raises this
Connected to iter_0008 findings on cross-asset dynamics
Four historical precedents: 1993-94 bond massacre, 2011 Italian BTP crisis, 2013 Taper Tantrum, 2022 UK gilt crisis — resolution timelines of 3-14 months
In 3 of 4 precedent episodes, bifurcation was resolved by policy intervention (fiscal consolidation or central bank backstop)
Short end reflects expected policy rate (Taylor Rule), long end reflects term premium (fiscal supply)
Debate pair_0 found the combined structural-empirical case 'materially stronger than either alone'
Six agents independently document and interpret the pattern across three analytical vocabularies
2Y approximates expected policy rate; 30Y approximates expected rate plus term premium
Front end transmits policy intent; back end transmits fiscal supply (rates_strategist_02)
Bianchi-Melosi fiscal theory extension of NK model (macro_strategist_01)
Falsifiable prediction endorsed by three agents: 2Y-SPX turning positive signals full fiscal dominance
Pattern survived scrutiny in pair_0, pair_1, and pair_2 debates
open_questions: What is the optimal maturity point for maximum hedge effectiveness?
Is the bifurcation stable across different macro regimes?
How does the bifurcation change during crisis periods?

Is the bifurcation a fiscal dominance signal or a transient phenomenon? Challenger_01 identified an internal contradiction: Fed cannot simultaneously be credible enough to anchor 2Y and impotent at 30Y
Alternative explanations: QT mechanics, supply-demand imbalance, foreign investor rebalancing, inflation risk premium
Without policy intervention, will the current bifurcation persist longer than any historical precedent?
Is the +0.05 to +0.15 long-end correlation statistically distinguishable from zero given the sample?
Is the bifurcation over-fitted to a 3-5 year sample with only 4 historical precedents (challenger_01)?
What would trigger 2Y-SPX turning positive, signaling full fiscal dominance?
